name: Bennu
description: The Bennu is an ancient Egyptian mythical bird associated with the sun, creation, and rebirth. Often linked with the god Ra, it is believed to have created itself from a fire that burned on one of the sacred trees in the temple of Ra at Heliopolis. The Bennu is considered the ba (soul) of Ra and Osiris, embodying the cyclical nature of existence and the promise of resurrection. Depictions of the Bennu often resemble a heron or eagle, characterized by brilliant plumage, often with golden or reddish hues, symbolizing the sun's radiant energy. The Bennu’s cry was said to initiate time itself. It was a symbol of hope, renewal, and the triumph over death, serving as a powerful emblem in Egyptian religious beliefs and funerary rites. Its association with Heliopolis, the city of the sun, further solidifies its connection to solar power and the continuous cycle of day and night, life and death.
subtitle: The Egyptian Bird of Rebirth
appearance: Resembles a large heron or eagle with bright golden, red, and purple plumage. It often has a long beak and a crest of two long feathers.
habitat: Heliopolis, Egypt; associated with the sacred trees in the temple of Ra.
behavior: The Bennu is a solitary and revered creature. It is said to appear during times of significant transition or renewal, announcing the dawn of a new era. Its presence is a sign of hope and divine favor.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Bennu
keyMyths.description: The Bennu is said to have created itself from a fire that burned on a sacred tree in the temple of Ra. This act of self-creation symbolizes rebirth and the cyclical nature of existence. It is a powerful representation of the sun's daily renewal.

keyMyths.title: The Bennu and Osiris
keyMyths.description: The Bennu is sometimes considered the ba (soul) of Osiris, the god of the afterlife. This connection links the Bennu to the themes of death, resurrection, and the eternal cycle of life. The Bennu's presence is a promise of continued existence beyond death.

keyMyths.title: The Periodic Appearance
keyMyths.description: Some accounts suggest the Bennu appeared only once every 500 years, returning to Heliopolis to renew itself at the altar of Ra. This rare appearance was a cause for great celebration and was seen as a sign of divine blessing and the continuation of cosmic order.

keyMyths.title: Association with the Phoenix
keyMyths.description: The Bennu bird is often linked to the Greek phoenix. Classical authors like Herodotus and Pliny the Elder identified the Bennu as the inspiration for the phoenix myth, emphasizing its ability to be reborn from ashes.

sources.author: Allen, James P.
sources.work: Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs
sources.description: Provides insights into Egyptian religious beliefs and symbolism associated with the Bennu.
sources.text: Provides insights into Egyptian religious beliefs and symbolism associated with the Bennu.

sources.author: Wilkinson, Richard H.
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Offers a comprehensive overview of the Bennu's role in the Egyptian pantheon and its connection to Ra and Osiris.
sources.text: Offers a comprehensive overview of the Bennu's role in the Egyptian pantheon and its connection to Ra and Osiris.

sources.author: Herodotus
sources.description: Describes the Bennu bird and its connection to the phoenix myth, providing historical context.
sources.text: Describes the Bennu bird and its connection to the phoenix myth, providing historical context.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Discusses the phoenix and its potential origins from the Egyptian Bennu bird, offering insights into cross-cultural mythological connections.
